title: Countries by income classification
subtitle: The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.
caption:
Countries by income classification

The World Bank's income classification divides countries into four categories based on their gross national income (GNI) per capita. Thresholds between income groups have changed over time.

This stacked area chart displays the count of countries within four income classifications from 1987 to 2024. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 250, representing the number of countries, while the horizontal axis marks years in five-year intervals. The data is segmented into four colored bands: low income (purple), lower-middle income (pink), upper-middle income (light green), and high income (dark green). In 1987, the low-income category begins at approximately 50 countries, rising to a peak of roughly 65 around 1995 before declining steadily to about 25 by 2024. Conversely, the high-income band starts near 40 countries in 1987, expands rapidly to over 60 by 1992, and continues a gradual ascent to occupy the top section of the stack, reaching approximately 85 countries by the end of the period. The lower-middle and upper-middle income groups fluctuate in the middle sections; the lower-middle band widens significantly between 1987 and 1994, reaching nearly 80 countries, then contracts to roughly 55 by 2024. The upper-middle band remains relatively stable in width, hovering between 30 and 40 countries throughout the timeline. The total height of the stacked areas rises from roughly 165 in 1987 to a plateau near 220 after 2010. As noted in the metadata, countries are grouped based on the income classification for each respective year, meaning group membership can change over time.
